steps.title: Identify the two independent clauses
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses: (1) the quoted Nobel criterion ending with "idealistic [blank]" and (2) "in 1912, for instance, judges recognized Gerhart Johann Robert Hauptmann..." The second clause illustrates the first, so they need punctuation that joins two independent clauses — a semicolon. Crucially, in American English a semicolon goes outside the closing quotation mark.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘direction"’: Closes the quote with no punctuation between the two independent clauses — creates a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘direction" and,’: Adds "and," which is grammatically awkward and does not properly join the clauses. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘direction";’: Closes the quote, then places the semicolon outside the quotation mark — correctly joining the two independent clauses per American English conventions. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘direction,"’: Places a comma inside the closing quotation mark, which cannot join two independent clauses and misapplies punctuation placement. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
